data.title: Another Strong Week For Stocks
data.description:
With the week now complete, below is a helpful look at the recent performance of various asset classes using key ETFs traded in the US.

Large-cap growth (IVW) was up 2.6% on the week, while mid-caps and small-caps actually fell. Technology (XLK) was the best-performing sector due to Oracle's (ORCL) massive gain, while Consumer Staples (XLP) and Materials (XLB) were the worst.

Outside of the US, there are a dozen country ETFs now up 20%+ on the year, which easily beats out SPY's year-to-date gain of 12.8%. Spain (EWP) is up the most in 2025 with a huge gain of 59.6%.

Argentina (ARGT) and natural gas (UNG) were the two worst-performing ETFs in our matrix this week. Sticking with commodities, agriculture (DBA), gold (GLD), and silver (SLV) all rallied more than 1%, with GLD and SLV extending huge year-to-date gains.

Finally, Treasury ETFs were also up across the board this week as rates fell, especially at the long end.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Consumer Sentiment Falls To 4-Month Low In September
data.description:
By Jennifer Nash

Consumer sentiment fell for a second straight month, hitting its lowest level since May. The Michigan Consumer Sentiment Index was down 2.8 points, or about 5%, to 55.4 in September. The latest reading was lower than the forecast of 58.2. Consumer sentiment has fallen by 21.0% (14.7 points) compared to a year ago.

The Michigan Consumer Sentiment Index is a monthly survey of consumer confidence levels in the U.S. with regard to the economy, personal finances, business conditions, and buying conditions, conducted by the University of Michigan. There are two reports released each month: a preliminary report released mid-month and a final report released at the end of the month.

Joanne Hsu, the director of surveys, made the following comments:

Consumer sentiment moved down less than three index points in early September. This month’s easing in economic views was particularly strong among lower and middle income consumers. Buying conditions for durables improved, while all other index components fell. Consumers continue to note multiple vulnerabilities in the economy, with rising risks to business conditions, labor markets, and inflation. Likewise, consumers perceive risks to their pocketbooks as well; current and expected personal finances both eased about 8% this month. Trade policy remains highly salient to consumers, with about 60% of consumers providing unprompted comments about tariffs during interviews, little changed from last month. Still, sentiment remains above April and May 2025 readings, immediately after the initial announcement of reciprocal tariffs.

See the chart below for a long-term perspective on this widely watched indicator. We've highlighted the index's value at the start of each recession and included a callout to the most recent 12 months. At 55.4, the current level is below the index's value at the start of all six recessions since its inception.

To put today’s report in historical context, consumer sentiment is currently 34.3% below its average reading of 84.3 (arithmetic mean) and 33.4% below its geometric mean of 83.1, based on data dating back to 1978. The current index level is at the 1st percentile of the 573 monthly data points in this series.

This indicator is somewhat volatile, with an average monthly change of 3.1 points. The latest data shows a 2.8-point change from the previous month. To visualize the volatility, here’s a chart with monthly data and a three-month moving average.

The bottom half of the chart includes real GDP, which allows us to evaluate the correlation between the Michigan Consumer Sentiment Index and the broader economy.

Each month, the survey results highlight sentiment within each political party. To provide additional context, I’ve included a chart that shows the presidencies over our timeframe. As you can see, sentiment has fluctuated both positively and negatively under both Republican and Democratic administrations.

Michigan Consumer Sentiment Index: Components

The Michigan Consumer Sentiment Index consists of two sub-indexes: the Current Economic Conditions Index (CECI) and the Consumer Expectations Index (CEI). The CECI reflects consumers' views of their current financial situation and the overall economy, while the CEI gauges their outlook for the future.

In September, the CECI fell for a second straight month to 61.2, its lowest level since May. This represents a 0.8% decline from the previous month and a 3.3% drop from a year ago. This reading was below the forecast of 61.3.

Meanwhile, the CEI fell for a third straight month to 51.8, its lowest level since May. This represents a 7.3% decline from the previous month and a 30.4% drop from one year ago. This reading was below the forecast of 54.9.

Michigan Consumer Sentiment Index: Inflation Expectations

Year-ahead inflation expectations held steady at 4.8%, unchanged from August. Long-run inflation expectations moved up for the second straight month to 3.9% in September. This current reading is considerably lower than the 4.4% seen in April.

Other Sentiment Indicators

For an additional perspective on consumer attitudes, see the most recent Conference Board's Consumer Confidence Index. Both indexes gauge consumer attitudes toward the current and future strength of the economy. However, the Consumer Confidence Index is more influenced by employment and labor market conditions, while the Michigan Sentiment Index is more focused on household finances and the impact of inflation.

The Conference Board index is the more volatile of the two, but the broad pattern and general trends have been remarkably similar to the Michigan index.

And finally, the prevailing mood of the Michigan survey is also similar to the mood of small business owners, as captured by the NFIB business optimism Index (monthly update here).

ETFs associated with sentiment include the Consumer Discretionary Select Sector SPDR Fund (XLY).

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: WTI Crude Oil Slips Below $62 As Supply Concerns Outweigh Demand Optimism
data.description:
By Jainam Mehta

​WTI crude oil futures are facing renewed pressure, sliding below $62 per barrel in Friday’s session and extending losses for a third day. The downturn comes after U.S. government data showed a surprise inventory build of 3.9 million barrels, defying expectations for a draw and raising doubts about the strength of domestic demand.

Global supply expectations have added to the bearish tone. The International Energy Agency projected stronger-than-anticipated output growth, largely from OPEC+, while OPEC itself left demand projections steady. The divergence highlights the uncertainty over whether markets can absorb additional flows without tipping into surplus.

Geopolitical factors remain in play. Washington is pressing G7 allies to impose harsher tariffs on India and China for their purchases of Russian crude, a move that could alter trade dynamics. Still, near-term balances remain dictated by inventory data and the pace of U.S. consumption, which has softened as refining margins narrow and gasoline demand cools after the summer peak.

Technical levels point to fragility

From a technical standpoint, WTI has broken down from its short-lived ascending channel that lifted prices earlier in September. The retreat through $63.50 and $63.10 has turned those levels into resistance.

Moving averages have rolled over, with the 20-, 50-, and 100-period EMAs now stacked overhead, reinforcing a bearish structure. The RSI at 43 shows momentum is weak but not yet oversold, leaving scope for additional declines.

Immediate support lies at $61.50, with the psychological $60 mark viewed as the critical level to maintain. A rebound above $63.50–$64.10 would be required to restore bullish momentum, while failure to defend $61 could open the path toward $59–$60.

Outlook

Despite this week’s weakness, WTI is still holding onto modest weekly gains, with earlier strength driven by geopolitical risks in the Middle East and Ukraine providing a floor. Yet unless disruptions escalate, the balance of risks remains tilted lower, with supply growth and fading U.S. demand overshadowing bullish catalysts.

In our previous analysis, we highlighted how WTI’s attempts to reclaim the mid-$60s were fragile without clear evidence of stronger consumption. The current breakdown validates that caution, placing greater importance on whether $60 can hold as the market’s next test.

This material may contain third-party opinions, none of the data and information on this webpage constitutes investment advice according to our Disclaimer. While we adhere to strict Editorial Integrity, this post may contain references to products from our partners.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: S&P 500 Earnings: 2026 Expected S&P 500 EPS Hasn't Wavered Much From Expected 14% Growth Rate
data.description:
If readers click on and expand the following spreadsheet, it shows the weekly progression of S&P 500 EPS estimates (since mid-April) for 2025, 2026 and 2027.

Usually in May of every year, this blog gives readers a look at the next year’s expected EPS growth rate for the S&P 500, but it was decided to wait this year, given Liberation Day, and now the Federal Reserve tiff, to see what the weekly revisions indicate.

Remarkably both 2025 and 2026 S&P 500 EPS are expecting 10% EPS growth in 2025 and 14% in 2026. If this EPS growth materializes, (2025 is less in doubt than 2026), this would be the best two-year rate of growth for S&P 500 earnings since 2017 and 2018’s +12% and +23% respective EPS growth from President Trump’s last term (excluding Covid or 2020 and 2021).

In 2021, S&P 500 EPS grew 49%, likely on the back of zero interest rates and the massive government stimulus passed by Congress in 2021. What’s interesting for Fed watchers this week is that S&P 500 EPS grew only 1% in 2019, which happened to be the year Jay Powell started reducing the fed funds rate after pushing the fed funds rate higher (Yellen and then Jay Powell) in late 2016.

Here’s a quick history of S&P 500 EPS growth:

  • 2027: $344.16 (estimate) expected 13% EPS growth
  • 2026: $304.59 (estimate) expected 14% EPS growth
  • 2025: $267.77 (estimate) expected 10% EPS growth
  • 2024: $242.73 (actual) 10% actual EPS growth
  • 2023: $221.36 (actual) 1% actual EPS growth
  • 2022: $218.09 (actual) 5% actual EPS growth rate
  • 2021: $208.12 (actual) 49% actual EPS growth
  • 2020: $139.72 (actual) -14% decline in S&P 500 EPS YoY

Source: LSEG

What readers might find fascinating is that 2020’s actual S&P 500 EPS of $139.72 compared to 2026’s expected $304.59 results in an annualized S&P 500 EPS growth rate of 13-14% over that 7-year period. If you are wondering what the back of this secular bull market was built on, there’s your answer.

There is another component too that matters, and that is “P/E expansion/contraction.” There’s a lot of talk about how the S&P 500 will trade next week with a 25 basis point rate cut.

For me, that only means the fed funds rate drops down to 4.125%, still above the 4% threshold. I've wondered if the FOMC would reduce the fed funds rate by .375 basis points to bring the fed funds rate to 4%.

The forward 4-quarter estimate (FFQE) for the S&P 500 this week was $284.02, up from $283.85 last week, for a forward P/E of 23.2x to end the week of 9/12/2025.

The S&P 500 “earnings yield” ended the week at 4.31%, still well below where I’d like to see it at 5%, which implies a 20x P/E for the S&P 500. There has only been one week in the last 11 that the S&P 500 FFQE has declined sequentially.

Everything is way overbought within the S&P 500. Don’t be surprised to see some selling next week. There is more hype than usual built into the FOMC meeting given the tension between the President, Treasury Secretary and Fed Chair Jay Powell.

Personally, I do think the FOMC could drop the fed funds rate 50 bps lower to 3.87%, and it wouldn’t likely ignite inflation expectations. Rick Rieder, BlackRock's major domo, made a very interesting comment in his monthly bond call Thursday morning, 9/11/25.

Rick mentioned that while the FOMC’s inflation objective is 2%, the Fed’s goal is “price stability.” Yes, Rick is in the candidate pool for the next Fed Chairman following Jay Powell’s term, so he might be playing to the so-called Washington crowd a bit, but he has a point, too.

None of this is advice or a recommendation, but only an opinion. Past performance is no guarantee of future results. All SP 500 EPS data is sourced from LSEG.com. The above information may or may not be updated and if updated, may not be done so in a timely fashion.

Thanks for reading.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Viavi: Growth Is Back, Cash Flow Is Real, And Spirent Changes The Game
data.description:
Thesis Snapshot

Back in June, I argued Viavi was turning the corner. Three months and a 27% rally later, it’d be fair to say that the numbers are doing the talking. Q4 closed out a year of steady execution: core segments posted double-digit YoY growth, gross margins expanded, and free cash flow is up to $62 million. The business has momentum, and more importantly, the path forward looks cleaner.

I don’t think that this is about getting in early anymore. The setup now is about staying long into FY26, where the Spirent acquisition adds scale, bookings strength in cloud and 5G continue to build, and Viavi is looking more like a cash-flow compounder than a recovery play. I’m recommending another Buy.

The Turning Point

In my view, Q4 FY25 was the quarter where everything finally started lining up. Both NSE and OSP grew double digits YoY: NSE posted 14.8% growth, led by demand across cloud, hyperscale, and next-gen fiber lab environments. OSP came in at 16.6%, with anti-counterfeiting and aerospace optics pulling most of the weight there. For Viavi Solutions Inc., a business that’s spent most of the past year treading water, this feels like real, grounded momentum.

Margins moved in the right direction, too. Non-GAAP gross margin hit 60.1% in Q4, slightly up from both the prior quarter and last year, while non-GAAP operating margin climbed 350bps sequentially. What stood out for me, though, was cash flow. Operating cash flow for the quarter was around $23.8 million, against $7.8 million in Q3. Free cash was more encouraging: $62 million for FY25, and ~$18 million for Q4 25. That doesn’t happen without tighter discipline and a better segment mix. And it tells me Viavi’s operating model is finally getting out of its own way.

Cash closed the year at $429 million, and that includes short-term investments and restricted cash. Long-term debt stands at $396.3 million, which is tied to the 3.75% 2029 notes. I think that’s a manageable profile, especially since Spirent’s not hitting the cash stack yet, but it’ll be worth watching how management balances integration and deleveraging across FY26.

Most importantly, the FY26 setup now looks meaningfully better than it did when I initiated coverage in June. The Spirent deal is locked in, bookings are picking up, and segment performance is no longer dragging. This is the shift I was waiting for.

Segment View: Where the Story Gets Interesting

Network & Service Enablement

This is where the turnaround is taking shape. NSE revenue rose 14.8% YoY and 2.0% QoQ in Q4, led by demand recovery across lab and production test, hyperscale networks, and government programs. It’s the most cyclical part of Viavi’s business, but orders in the quarter were up, and with Spirent integration coming online in FY26, this segment is about to get both deeper and wider.

To be clear, I’m not calling this a finished product, but I think FY26 gives NSE a clean shot at operating leverage if volumes hold up and Spirent folds in efficiently.

Optical Security & Performance Products

OSP is still doing what it does best: quietly drive high-margin growth while everyone’s watching NSE. The segment grew 16.6% YoY, led by strong performance in anti-counterfeiting pigments and aerospace/defense optics. I’d say that Viavi’s ability to hold share in such a niche market with relatively stable demand cycles gives this unit a level of consistency that the rest of the business can’t always match.

And the margins are still excellent, especially the operating margin, which was over 30% again this quarter, so it’s going to remain a reliable contributor to consolidated profitability. I expect OSP to keep pace with expectations, especially with global security demand and 3D sensing still running in the background.

Integration Watch: Spirent Sets the Next Stage

Now that the $425 million acquisition of Spirent’s HSE, network security, and CE businesses is very close to being completed, the real work begins. And for me, this is where Viavi either levels up or drags its momentum into the mud.

The deal involves Viavi buying Spirent's "high-speed ethernet and network security business lines from Keysight Technologies, Inc. for $410 million base cash consideration and an additional $15 million contingent cash consideration to be paid at closing."

Spirent brings obvious benefits: expanded reach in network testing, 5G core, automation, and even cybersecurity assurance, but none of that matters if Viavi fumbles the integration. Management didn’t say much besides when the deal should close, end of September, but we can expect FY26 to be a year of transition. That’s fine, but ideally, I’d like to know some of their synergy targets.

I’ll be watching:

  • How quickly Spirent’s higher-margin software and services mix starts impacting Viavi’s consolidated profile.

  • Whether they can hold or grow operating margins while folding in a full global business.

  • And whether cross-selling opportunities, especially in lab automation and assurance, show up in segment bookings by Q2/Q3 next year.

Clearly, the upside is real. If Spirent gets absorbed cleanly, Viavi becomes a stronger recurring revenue story with a broader TAM and a clearer tech narrative. But it’s still a big ask. It’s very difficult to pull off a clean integration of this size, and any stumble will show up fast, either in cost bloat, delayed growth, or execution drag across NSE.

For now, though, I’m giving management the benefit of the doubt.

Valuation & Setup Into FY26

At ~2.5x EV/Sales, Viavi isn’t cheap, but it’s still not priced like a growth story either.

The business outperformed last year, when it barely crossed the $1 billion revenue mark, swung to $62 million in free cash flow, and finally posted double-digit growth in both major segments, but the stock still trades like the turnaround hasn’t stuck. I don’t think anyone's assigning a premium to the Spirent story yet, even though that business brings a higher-margin mix, more automation and software hooks, and expands Viavi’s footprint into assurance and cybersecurity. That tells me the rerate isn’t quite baked in.

To be clear, I’m not betting on multiple expansion for its own sake, but if margins hold and integration is clean, the math gets easier. Spirent adds scale, and scale here means operating leverage. Viavi’s already pushed gross margin to 60.1%, and non-GAAP operating margin’s creeping up, even before any synergy work starts to hit. That gives the setup for FY26 a floor, and possibly a springboard.

Risks

There are still risks, of course. For one, wireless infrastructure demand is still weak, and Viavi isn’t fully insulated. That softness could bleed into NSE bookings if carriers and NEMs stay cautious. Also, OSP is stable, but it is also lumpy by nature, and any drop in defense or anti-counterfeit pigment orders can hit margins quickly. When we add in $396 million in debt (along with a $600 million Term Loan B) and a major integration underway, the margin for error is much, much tighter.

I’m also not ignoring the fact that Viavi needs to prove that it can absorb something as big as the Spirent business units without friction. Execution risk is real. If synergies take longer or if cost bloat creeps in, the narrative can slip back to Viavi being a low-growth test gear company with a slightly bloated SG&A line.

Your Takeaway

This is the first time in over two years that Viavi has momentum in both segments, a real cash flow profile, and a clear forward setup with Spirent. The valuation is nicely positioned, in my view, the margins are trending the right way, and management is keeping a tight grip on the narrative. Consequently, I believe that if the company keeps stringing these quarters together, a rerate is coming.

data.title: Canada's Big Banks Sidestep Tariff Fallout - What Investors Should Watch For Next
data.description:
Canadian lenders turned in another strong quarter, with almost all the major banks beating expectations. Mario Mendonca, Managing Director at TD Cowen, speaks with MoneyTalk’s Kim Parlee about what he’s looking for in coming quarters and why bank valuations may be a little stretched.

Transcript

Kim Parlee: Mario, I want to start with just an overview of all the banks in terms of what you saw. Thematically, what really stood out for you this quarter?

Mario Mendonca: It was an important quarter for the banks. If you had focused on our banks earlier this year, as I did, you would have concluded that the year was going to be a tough year. With all the stuff around tariffs and USMCA early in the year, you would have concluded, as I did, this would have been a challenging year. Economic growth would have been weaker. But in fact, nothing's played out that way.

USMCA has shielded most Canadian exports into the US. Canadian government support is there, or at least it's promised to be there. Rates are a little lower. All of that has really helped to support the Canadian banks. So when I look at the quarter, in terms of broad strokes, EPS up 16% year over year, very good quarter.

Kim Parlee: And you were predicting 8, right, I think?

Mario Mendonca: Around 8. Yeah. So a clear beat. Average for our banks over the long term is around 7% or 8%, so a clear beat. Now, lower PCLs are a big part of that, but it wasn't just credit. Overall pretax preprovision or precredit growth was 18%. And that was 6% better than we were expecting.

Now, importantly, capital markets played a very big role in driving that. And I think some folks would refer to that as lower quality. But nonetheless, it was still there. Revenue was very strong in capital markets. It drove operating leverage of 3.9%. Again, well above 1% is around average for our Canadian banks. So in every respect, it was a good solid quarter for the banks.

But below the earnings, what really drove the quarter and made it sound especially good for me is what the banks are saying on credit. The banks are saying that credit losses are stabilizing from here, not getting worse. Now, that could change. If we get a bad result on USMCA, or US economic growth falters, or Canadian economic growth falters, the banks will have to retract that.

But as of right now, the banks are telling us that credit is stabilizing. And it became clear in unsecured lending, unsecured lending, which is where the risk really is, credit cards, auto lending, unsecured lines of credit. That's stabilized relative to previous quarters. So yeah, all the indicators are pretty good. Now, there's a lot of other economic data that's behind the scenes. But for the quarter, all the themes were positive.

Kim Parlee: Let me ask you, we'll get into the individual names in a second, but when you think about some of these other shoes that can drop, USMCA or recession, things changing, how are you going to be thinking about -- what are you going to be looking for to understand what's going to have the biggest impact?

Mario Mendonca: Well, unfortunately, so much of it depends on, and it's become binary, is USMCA resolved in a quick, amicable way. That seems unlikely. Because of the characters involved and their penchant for headlines, it seems unlikely to me that this is going to be a nice, easy settlement with -- the USMCA, I'm referring to.

I suspect that over the next few months, we're going to get a lot of threats, a lot of headlines. And what we're going to have to decide for ourselves is whether we take those seriously. Or do we just assume this is more negotiating? The market so far has been absolutely correct to ignore most of the saber rattling that we've heard from the US.

I suspect that's what we're going to get a lot more of that. I hope the market is right and that we can continue to ignore a lot of the threats. But that's the big one. What happens to US unemployment, US job growth, Canadian job growth? Those are the big ones I'm focusing on.

Kim Parlee: Which has really ticked down.

Mario Mendonca: It has. It's slowed. It's a little bit uncomfortable because everything was firing along really, really well. And now we're seeing some weak data emerge in the US. That will affect loan growth, could affect credit, could affect capital markets. Those are the big ones I'm focused on.

Kim Parlee: And in light of that, valuations on Canadian banks right now are stretched. So maybe just give us the historical picture versus where we are now.

Mario Mendonca: If you look at the last 25 years, as I do, because that's around about when I came into the industry, when I really paid attention, break that up into 1,300 weeks or so in that period, roughly 66% of the time, the Canadian banks trade -- on a forward basis, trade between 10 and 12 times earnings.

Right now, on 2026, which is forward earnings, they're around 12.5, so a little bit above average. You can also look at things like price to book, the equity risk premium. All of them will say the same thing -- the banks look a little stretched. Now, a stretch valuation in the context of USMCA, US unemployment, the uncertainty, geopolitical uncertainty, you'd say, wow, these things look stretched. Why are we buying them now?

There's no shortage of investors that take a different view. One view is, why are you looking at 2026? Growth will accelerate really well into 2027. And on 2027, they trade below 12. There's no shortage of investors that are willing to look completely through 2026, which is a little surprising, that we're going to ignore all of next year and the risks of next year. But there are investors that do that.

There's another group of investors that take even more bullish outlook and say, AI is changing the world. Canadian banks and banks globally will see their expense ratios decline sharply over the next five years. Banks are a play on AI, so why are you fussed about near-term valuation? And I hear that all the time.

So I apply a very traditionalist view to this. I look at those valuations and I'm uncomfortable when they go through 12. But there's no shortage of investors that do not care.

Kim Parlee: Speaking of those shortage of investors and no shortage of investors, the FDI net inflows chart was pretty interesting. Tell me about that.

Mario Mendonca: Foreign direct investment. So it's been many -- it's been a decade since we've seen money flow into Canada regularly, foreign direct investment. And over the last couple of months -- I believe this is one that tracks it on a historical basis -- we've seen money flowing into Canada. Investors are buying in Canada.

Why is that? Has it become sort of discomfort with the US? Is the US just not the reliable place it used to be? Is it US deficits? We've seen what's happened to US long bond yields moving higher. So is it possible that there's a discomfort in the US that's bringing money to Canada? It could be.

When I speak to investors globally-- and I have been out and about. I've been to London recently, New York recently, and I speak to investors about Canada generally. They're pretty optimistic, oddly. They're optimistic about our Canadian banks and Canadian investing.

Kim Parlee: Based on?

Mario Mendonca: I think sometimes it's a comparison to the US. I think there's a possibility that some folks look at the US as not the stable long-term play it used to be.

Kim Parlee: OK. Let's talk about the inside the sector, some specific names. And you tweaked some of, it looks like, your earnings estimates and your price targets on the bank that we went through. Yes, all higher except for one, I think. Let's go through the buys that you have right now. So Bank of Nova Scotia (BNS, BNS:CA) and CIBC (CM, CM:CA), you have a buy on both those banks. Let's start with Scotiabank. What do you like? What are you worried about?

Mario Mendonca: So those two calls really worked well this quarter. Now, I've been a buy on Scotia since November of 2024. And I thought 2025 was going to be the year when Scotia revealed that they were returning to growth in international. It did not happen until this quarter. This quarter, Scotia was clear with the street that this long period of optimization is coming to an end.

And optimization is a euphemism for shrinking. We're shrinking. We're getting out of certain relationships and loans we don't like. Scotia is telling us that they're about done there. And if that's true, we're going to see growth in international lending, Canadian commercial, US corporate. When you layer on top of that that Scotia's credit loss should likely come down fairly abruptly next year, Scotia looks like a pretty good grower in '26 and '27.

But most importantly, you've got a valuation that is so low relative to the peer group. So I like that combination-- a really low valuation when we're clearly seeing an inflection point in growth. That's why Scotia worked so well recently.

Kim Parlee: OK. And then what about CIBC?

Mario Mendonca: CIBC is one of those names -- it was about two years ago, maybe two and a half years ago, I upgraded the stock, made it our top pick. And I appreciate that there have been plenty of bad calls in my career, but this was not one of them. This was one that really, really worked. CIBC is the best performer over the last couple of years.

It has gone from a bank that we all looked at as most likely to step on a rake. And they've really delivered. And I'll give it to that management team, Victor Dodig, he's retiring. His last conference call was just last week, and it was a real love. And everybody was congratulating him on a really good career. And he did. He had a solid career.

And he took CIBC from an underperformer to at least average. And that's a big deal to me. So CIBC, strong pretax, preprovision profit growth. They're not blowing up on credit. And historically, that's what CIBC would do. They would find a way to hurt themselves on credit. So really, it's down the fairway performance--

Kim Parlee: And new management, I would expect.

Mario Mendonca: New management. You mean, in terms of--

Kim Parlee: Yeah, coming in as they-- yeah, yeah.

Mario Mendonca: Yeah, for sure. Victor Dodig, the folks that he put in charge of domestic retail, the CFO, these are all important moves. And so CIBC, I think in the past, had swung for the fences and struck out. The bank now is much more client-focused, middle of the fairway. And that's why the market loves them.

Kim Parlee: OK, let's go to the holds. BMO (BMO, BMO:CA).

Mario Mendonca: BMO is an interesting one. The market really likes BMO. They're a US exposure. I think that's what the market favors. BMO strikes me as having some near-term risk. And I'll explain it this way. The market is looking for BMO to deliver very strong earnings growth in 2026. And it's coming on the belief that outsized credit losses in prior years will be mitigated in 2026.

A more normal credit picture, maybe BMO with 35 basis points of credit losses, will drive very strong growth in 2026. When you speak to management, however, they're cautioning us that PCLs or credit losses could remain at this elevated level well into 2026. When you ask them specifically why, why would you say credit could remain elevated going to '26, they'll point you specifically to unsecured consumer lending and the bank's overweight to the mass affluent as opposed to the affluent, say, a Royal (RY, RY:CA), for example.

So BMO is warning the street that credit could remain higher. Analysts and investors are talking about how credit is going to get much lower in 2026. That disconnect creates a risk as we get into Q4 '25 for BMO.

Kim Parlee: National Bank (OTCPK:NTIOF, NA:CA), it looks to me as though it's the only one you actually didn't change the price target when they came out and still have a hold on this one.

Mario Mendonca: See, I felt for some time now that National was trading at roughly the same valuation at Royal, and it is not a Royal Bank. Their business mix is not favorable. Very overweight capital markets, particularly trading. An overweight or a fairly sizable position in Cambodia, where gross impaired losses have materially moved higher, moved materially higher.

So I look National Bank and I just don't believe investors should trade at the premium. It was trading at the same valuation as Royal. This was a rough quarter for National, where we saw gross impaired losses continue to move higher in Cambodia. And we saw the vulnerability of their trading book.

So investors rightfully took National Bank from a premium back down to a more average valuation, which I think is great because it sets them up for a very good Q4. What National Bank can do in Q4 is talk about expense synergies on CWB, funding synergies on CWB, the benefits of converting CWB from standardized to advanced on their capital, which produces a lot of capital for National Bank.

So National has a great opportunity to impress the street in Q4 '25 with a valuation that looks reasonable. So yeah, I felt no need to raise estimates or target prices on National Bank because in my view, it was overvalued to start with.

Kim Parlee: And finally, Royal, which you did, you upped, I guess, to 208 from 191, but still a hold.

Mario Mendonca: Yeah, see, Royal was one of those interesting stocks. So in November 2024, I downgraded Royal, which is unusual for a Canadian bank analyst to do. Everybody always loves Royal. Downgrading Royal was not an indictment on Royal's performance, but it was just the valuation. It was trading at a very significant premium to the group.

If you look at a chart now of Royal's premium relative to the group, it has collapsed. It no longer trades at a meaningful premium. It's still a premium but not a meaningful premium. So it is quite easy for me to become a little bit more optimistic on Royal. I'm looking for a better entry point. And ultimately, if I'm not bullish on the group, I don't want to have too many buys on the group. So Royal just doesn't fit in. But after Scotia and CIBC, Royal would be my next pick.

Kim Parlee: One last question for you. For someone who's watching, and a lot of Canadian investors own a lot of these stocks, what have we not talked about that you think people need to pay attention to as they go forward?

Mario Mendonca: I think as we think about 2026, what I'd ask investors to think about the most is, how do you feel about the world going into 2026? How do you feel about the Canadian economy or the US economy? How do you feel about USMCA? Do you think it'll be effortless, it'll be smooth sailing? How do you feel about unemployment?

And if your view is that, going into 2026, things feel a little uncertain, a little uncertain about trade, a little uncertain about unemployment, and a little uncertain about the macro picture, then reflect on this -- Canadian banks are nothing if they're not just a reflection of the economy. And if you're uncomfortable about how things are shaping up for 2026, then you might want to be a little uncomfortable about Canadian banks as well.

And in that context, they're trading at a premium valuation. That, to me, is the whole ballgame. If you're a little uncomfortable with the macro picture, you might want to be a little bit uncomfortable with the Canadian bank setting in '26.

Original Post

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Rentokil: Critical Assessment Of Recent Turnaround Successes
data.description:
Introduction

It has been quite a while since I last covered the multinational pest control and hygiene company Rentokil Initial plc (NYSE:RTO, OTCPK:RKLIF) here on Seeking Alpha, in February 2025, when I compared it to its main competitor in the U.S. – Rollins, Inc. (ROL).

Since my last article, in which I also pointed out first signs of a trend reversal in the U.S., the company has published its annual report for 2024 and its results for the first half of 2025. The former is important because it contains eagerly awaited information on the cash flow conversion rate and customer and staff retention statistics – in my view, these are extremely important indicators for assessing Rentokil's underlying operational quality in general. The latter are good indicators for assessing whether the situation has continued to improve, particularly in the U.S. As a reminder, Rentokil acquired Terminix Global Holdings, Inc. in October 2022, and management fell well short of initial expectations for the merger, which made the combined company the largest pest control business in the U.S.

In this update, I would therefore like to review the above-mentioned performance indicators and share my assessment of the information that has become available since my last article in February. Finally, I will explain whether the latest results have led to a change in my previously expressed reduced interest in a position in RTO shares, or whether the company has been removed from my watch list.

Rentokil's Free Cash Flow Performance In 2024 And So Far In 2025

In my previous article, I emphasized that I would focus primarily on the development of free cash flow in 2024. After all, it is the lifeblood of every company, with the exception of those that are dependent on regular capital increases.

Rentokil's free cash flow for 2024 was rather disappointing, even if Figure 1 suggests otherwise. When considering the seemingly excellent long-term growth, the acquisition of Terminix at the end of 2022 should be taken into account.

However, before we go into detail, two side issues should be considered.

First, from a cash flow perspective, the consolidation of Terminix only became visible in 2023, as the cash flow statement takes into account net cash flows over a reporting period. Second, I usually smooth out working capital movements using a variable-duration moving average. In this case, however, I have refrained from doing so, as such an adjustment would mix the impact of Rentokil's working capital movements before and after the merger with Terminix. Nevertheless, as always, I have accounted for stock-based compensation as a cash expenditure and, in this case, also made adjustments for lease-related cash flows, which are reported as cash flows from financing activities.

It was, of course, rather disappointing to see free cash flow decline by 13% to just £313 million in 2024. The decline is largely attributable to an increase in working capital compared to the previous year, but also to lower reported operating earnings.

It should be noted that although operating profit was indeed lower year-over-year, this was not due to goodwill impairment charges, for example, as these are added back to operating cash flow due to their non-cash nature. Nevertheless, it seems worth mentioning that goodwill impairment charges amounted to £28 million in 2024, compared to £3 million in 2023.

I attribute the decline in Rentokil's actual operating profit to significant restructuring and integration costs (page 54, 2024 annual report) on the one hand, and unfavorable exchange rate developments (page 59) on the other.

The red line in Figure 1 shows Rentokil's excess cash margin, a straightforward indicator of the sustainability of earnings sustainability in terms of their conversion into cash. In the case at hand, a declining percentage suggests that earnings are either growing faster (or declining more slowly) than operating cash flow, indicating difficulties in cash conversion, particularly since the acquisition of Terminix.

Rentokil's ongoing problems are also evident from a broader profitability perspective, as shown in Figure 2 by the deviation between actual and adjusted operating margin on the one hand and the once again weaker free cash flow margin of most recently less than 6% on the other.

Understandably, Rentokil's persistently weak profitability - which is partly due to the ongoing integration of Terminix and restructuring efforts - is also the reason for the disappointing returns on invested capital. Of course, there are several approaches to calculating the denominator of ROIC and CROIC (free cash flow-based ROIC), but all arrive at a similar CROIC of only about 3% for 2024 - which is definitely well below any reasonable estimate of the company's cost of equity.

Taken together, cash conversion efficiency and profitability in general are certainly aspects that should be closely monitored in the long term. However, it should be noted that 2025 is off to a good start, with Rentokil generating free cash flow of £282 million in the first half of 2025 on a reported basis. Of course, timing effects are most likely at play here, and I would not suggest to simply double the figure to estimate free cash flow for the full year. It is also worth noting that the free cash flow reported by management is usually higher than my own somewhat more conservative figures. However, assuming that management has indeed presented a comparable comparison, the year-over-year increase of more than 30% is certainly a positive sign.

In any case, the full-year results for 2025 will be an important milestone in assessing whether Terminix is finally on track to be properly integrated into Rentokil Initial. In this context, it should be noted that Rentokil's management indicated on page 18 of the 2024 annual report that the integration process for Terminix will take until the end of 2026 (!). Most recently, management even pointed out that despite the expected completion of the integration by the end of 2026 (as reflected by a segment margin of >20%), this "may mean not all branches are fully integrated by that time" (page 1, H1 2025 results press release). This tells me that a certain amount of skepticism is entirely justified, and I therefore maintain my rather cautious stance with respect to an investment in Rentokil from this perspective.

Personnel And Customer Retention Statistics

As I explained in detail in my previous report on Rentokil, strong personnel and customer retention are fundamental to sustainable operations. This is particularly true given the high degree of fragmentation in the pest control market. What made me rather cautious when I first took a closer look at Rentokil was the decline in personnel retention statistics since 2020. In addition, the consolidation of Terminix led to even weaker figures compared to when looking at Rentokil's performance in 2022 prior to the acquisition.

However, 2022 appears to have indeed marked the low point, as shown in Figure 3. Both sales and service staff retention recovered in 2023 and again in 2024, by a convincing 4.6 percentage points (%pt) and 2.3 %pt, respectively. Overall, staff retention improved by 2.4 %pt to 86.6% in 2024 and by 1.1 %pt to 87.0% in the first half of 2025 compared to the previous year. I was also quite impressed by the 4.2 %pt. improvement in the North America segment. However, the overall employee retention rate of still below 80% in this segment indicates that there is considerable room for improvement. That said, the results for 2025 so far are also promising in this regard, given the increase of 2.9% pt year-over-year to 80.7%.

These are clearly positive signs, even though I believe it is still a little early, as personnel compensation likely played a significant role in the reported improvements - especially in light of the once again weaker profitability.

The customer retention rate also developed positively in 2024 (increase of 0.5 %pt to 82.8%, Figure 4), suggesting that the recent increase in marketing expenditure is paying off. While management has not reported the customer retention rate for the first half of 2025 on a group-wide level, the retention rate in the international business (all regions outside North America) looks very promising at 85.2% (see slide 1, H1 2025 results presentation), as does the improvement in North America of 0.7 %pt to 80.5% (see slide 5 of the presentation). There is certainly room for improvement here too, and competition from the extremely well-run peer Rollins, Inc. in particular should not be underestimated, but I think Rentokil is definitely on the right track.

Taken together, Rentokil's management is clearly moving in the right direction and improving the fundamentals of what is now the largest pest control business in the U.S. Both current personnel and customer retention statistics are making me increasingly optimistic (after the somewhat sobering results in 2023), although there is still much to be done and competition remains fierce. Furthermore, it will be important to see whether Rentokil can break away from the current inverse correlation between retention rates and profitability, which suggests that the higher spending plays an important role in the current trend reversal.

The recently announced divestiture of the workwear business unit in France - which I never understood in terms of synergies, although I am aware that it is part of Rentokil's longer-term history - is, in my opinion, also a step in the right direction. Focusing on the company's core business is extremely important, especially given the ongoing difficulties in integrating the rather bold acquisition of Terminix. The comments on slide 11 of the H1 2025 presentation show that the workwear business was a significant driver of capital expenditures. Hence, the sale can be seen as a positive also from a cash flow profitability perspective. In addition, the expected net cash proceeds of $420 million are certainly also welcome, given the still relatively high debt of $4.22 billion. At the end of June 2025, Rentokil reported a leverage ratio (net debt to adjusted EBITDA) of 2.8x, and relative to 2024 adjusted free cash flow, the leverage ratio would be almost 10x.

As an aside, for those wondering why I switched to U.S. dollars in the discussion: Rentokil previously announced the intention to change its reporting currency to U.S. dollars from 2025, which makes sense from a regional segment size perspective but could nevertheless make year-over-year comparisons somewhat more complicated, at least in the short term.

In this context, I expressed my skepticism in my previous coverage of the company, mainly due to changes in certain reporting practices. For example, it took me some time to determine reasonably meaningful figures for Rentokil's organic revenue growth on a company level and for the North America segment following the acquisition of Terminix (Figure 5). Finally, and I admit that this is somewhat out of context, the moderate acceleration in organic growth rate in the second quarter of 2025, both in North America and on a company-wide basis, is another sign in the right direction. As shown below, organic revenue growth accelerated to 1.4% in the second quarter of 2025 from 0.7% in the first quarter of 2025 and is up 0.4 %pt year-over-year. On a consolidated basis, organic revenue growth remained unchanged in the second quarter compared to the first quarter, at 1.6% year-over-year.

Concluding Remarks

After a rather sobering 2023 and having read that the integration of Terminix would not be complete until the end of 2026 (and even by then, not all branches might be fully integrated), I did not have high expectations for the 2024 annual report and the recently published results for the first half of 2025.

In fact, free cash flow actually was rather disappointing, resulting in a cash return on invested capital that was - once again - well below any reasonable estimate of the company's cost of equity. Clearly, capital allocation is not a strength of Rentokil's management, although part of the disappointing cash conversion (and thus CROIC) was due to increased marketing expenses and improved personnel compensation. However, it should be highlighted in a positive context that reported free cash flow improved significantly in the first half of 2025, so I am eagerly awaiting the results for the full year.

As discussed in this update, there are several promising signs that the turnaround in Rentokil's North America segment (which consists largely of Terminix, acquired in late 2022) is taking hold. While increased marketing spend and staff compensation have likely played an important role in improving staff and customer retention metrics, I would not go so far as to say that Rentokil's management has embarked on an unsustainable path here.

While the continued decline in reported operating profit suggests this, strengthening the Rentokil and Terminix brands should definitely pay off in the long term, even with associated marketing expenses normalizing in the future. In addition, integration and restructuring costs are expected to disappear, particularly after 2026, which will also contribute to profitability. If efficiency and marketing measures are implemented appropriately also over the long run, the North America business should indeed be able to achieve operating profitability of more than 20%, similar to the extremely well-managed competitor Rollins, Inc., which I analyzed in my comparative analysis in February.

The sale of the French workwear business was also a step in the right direction, in my view, primarily because it frees up management resources to focus on the core business (pest control accounts for 80% of consolidated revenues after the sale), but also because it removed what appeared to be a significant driver of capital expenditures. Finally, the cash proceeds contribute to debt reduction, which is important given the still high level of debt, the expected continuation of the acquisition-oriented growth strategy, and, finally, the potentially somewhat sticky long-term interest rates (despite expected interest rate cuts).

Nevertheless, despite the recent and undoubtedly positive developments, I remain cautious here, mainly due to the general overly optimistic sentiment among investors (measured e.g. by the Shiller P/E ratio and several components of the Fear & Greed Index), the difficult macroeconomic outlook, and the consequently somewhat expected broader market correction. Since my last article, Rentokil shares appear to have gained slightly in value from a U.S. dollar perspective, while falling slightly on the London Stock Exchange (i.e. in GBX). Admittedly, since management switched to the U.S. dollar as its reporting currency at the beginning of 2025, one could argue that it makes more sense to track the price of Rentokil's ADRs which trade on the NYSE. However, as I generally invest for the long term, I do not attach too much importance to this aspect.

I could imagine opening a small position below £3.50, a price at which Rentokil shares appear to have found adequate support and which adequately recognizes the recent successes of management. However, I would like to emphasize that Rentokil is by no means cheap: even after the implied decline of around 10% from the current level, the price still corresponds to a forward P/E ratio of 20 and a free cash flow yield of only 3.5% (or 3.8% if we take the 2023-2024 average after the acquisition of Terminix as a basis). Finally, the announcement of an unchanged interim dividend together with the results for the first half of 2025 gave me pause – especially considering that management had recently reaffirmed its progressive dividend policy (Figure 6).

Thank you very much for reading my latest article. Whether you agree or disagree with my conclusions, I always welcome your opinion and feedback in the comments below. And if there's anything I should improve or expand on in future articles, drop me a line as well. As always, please consider this article only as a first step in your own due diligence.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

Introduction

However, since this list is only produced once per month, the data in it can quickly get out of date. Furthermore, with around 700 companies on the list, the sheer amount of data can quickly become overwhelming. I hope you found this article useful. Please let me know if you have any ideas for improving the format or data included in this series.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Fifth Third Bancorp Series I: Attractively Balanced Risk Reward With This 8% Yielder
data.description:
Thesis

Fifth Third Bancorp (FITB) is a bank out of Cincinnati, Ohio. Fifth Third Bancorp was founded in 1858, and it currently has a market capitalization of $30 billion, while its balance sheet has assets of over $200 billion.

Just like any bank, FITB employs a complex funding program, which includes debt, equity, and preferred equity. In today's article, we are going to focus on the Series I Preferred Shares (FITBI).

Balance sheet and capital snapshot

Firstly, Fifth Third Bancorp is considered a regional bank, serving customers within a specific region in the US. The bank is headquartered in Cincinnati and has a significant presence across the Midwest and Southeast United States, with a network of branches in states like Ohio, Kentucky, Indiana, Michigan, Illinois, etc.

As per the Seeking Alpha Financials tab, the bank has a balance sheet of over $200 billion, with over $160 billion in deposits. $42 billion of the deposit figure is represented by non-interest-bearing deposits. Fifth Third is rated investment grade by all major rating agencies:

A high rating is important when accessing cheap funding, although, via their leveraged nature, banks tend to move quickly from solvent to insolvent, as seen in the 2023 regional banking crisis.

When looking at a bank, it is important to assess its standing via capital ratios and, more importantly, the historic performance of such ratios. A well-run bank will have consistently high capital ratios, with only small occasional 'blips':

FITB has been fairly consistent at maintaining a CET-1 ratio of roughly 10% in the past years, with the minimum regulatory ratio at 4.5%.

Also, please note that because the bank has more than $100 billion in assets, it is subject to increased regulatory scrutiny as per the enhanced prudential standards:

The FRB’s capital plan rule requires BHCs with $100 billion or more in consolidated assets, including the Bancorp, to develop and maintain a capital plan approved by the Board of Directors on an annual basis. The mandatory elements of the capital plan are an assessment of the expected uses and sources of capital over a nine-quarter planning horizon, a description of all planned capital actions over the planning horizon, a discussion of any expected changes to the BHC’s business plan that are likely to have a material impact on its capital adequacy or liquidity, a detailed description of the BHC’s process for assessing capital adequacy and the BHC’s capital policy. (excerpt from the company's 2024 Annual Report)

Increased regulatory scrutiny means shareholders have additional checks and balances in respect to the risk run by the bank and its risk mitigation strategies. Please note that the Economic Growth, Regulatory Relief, and Consumer Protection Act (EGRRCPA) of 2018 exempted most banks with assets between $50 billion and $100 billion from the most stringent enhanced prudential standards but did not impact FITB due to its size.

Tricolor subprime lender event

Just like any bank, Fifth Third is a lending institution and thus subject to the occasional large loss due to unforeseen circumstances. A notable recent development is the default of auto lender Tricolor:

(Bloomberg - Sept 10, 2025) -- JPMorgan Chase & Co., Fifth Third Bancorp, and Barclays Plc are among banks bracing for potentially hundreds of millions of dollars in combined losses from loans tied to subprime auto lender Tricolor Holdings, according to people with knowledge of the matter.

Fifth Third said in a regulatory filing Tuesday that it faced an impairment charge of as much as $200 million after discovering alleged fraudulent activity at a commercial borrower, which it didn’t identify. The bank said it was working with law enforcement authorities.

Sudden impairments like the $200 million mentioned above result in lower profitability and a lower capital ratio, all else equal. We expect the lending institution to continue to set capital aside to have a ratio in excess of 10%, but it is important to note the importance of the CET-1 ratio in absorbing unforeseen losses.

Capital markets performance

Let us also look at how the capital markets have treated FITB in the past few years, since this is a good snapshot into what the market thinks regarding the quality of the assets and the institution as a whole:

We are comparing the institution to the Invesco KBW Bank ETF (KBWB), the regional powerhouse M&T Bank Corp. (MTB), and the gold standard for megabanks, namely JPMorgan (JPM). FITB has done very well in the past five years, beating KBWB and MTB. Furthermore, its drawdown in 2023 was in line with what we saw for the regional ETF and MTB. The market has a neutral to favorable view of the institution when it comes to pricing its equity.

Preferred shares

As part of its capital and buffers, the bank has issued preferred equity. As we saw from the regional banks crisis, a bank run usually ends up in a default and a complete wipeout of common equity, preferred equity, and most of the senior bonds as well. We wrote two distinct articles on this topic:

  • First Republic: When Preferred Equity Goes To Zero, Part 2
  • Signature Bank: When Preferred Equity Goes To Zero

Make no mistake about it, if there is a significant credit event in respect to FITB, expect the preferred equity to potentially be wiped out in conjunction with the common equity.

That being said, we have a number of issuances from FITB:

FITBI is a fixed-to-floating issuance that now resets to a floating rate of 3-month SOFR plus 0.261% plus 3.71%. The initial call date was in December 2023. While the current yield is high at 8.17%, we do not think these securities will get called.

Call risk and yield considerations

As stated in the above section, the current yield is 8.17%, which is on the high side. However, please note that the securities are floating rate, and the forward curve indicates lower Fed Funds and SOFR. Simply put, there is a 1-to-1 correlation to Fed Funds here. A decrease of 100 bps in Fed Funds will see SOFR lower by 100 bps and FITBI's yield decrease proportionately.

In fact, many banks like floating-rate debt because many of their assets are floating, and they do not need to do any additional asset-liability matching. We are of the opinion that FITBI will not be called unless there is a significant increase in the credit rating for FITB or a merger that will see a much larger institution that can issue preferred equity cheaply.

While we do not think the call risk is there, holders will still be subject to downward pressure from a recession or another banking crisis:

The 2023 regional banking crisis saw a -15% drawdown in the securities, while all other recent events only saw -5% drawdowns.

Conclusion

FITBI represents preferred securities from Fifth Third and is perpetual in nature. The current yield is high at 8.17% given the investment grade rating for the institution, but the shares are floating rate. As Fed Funds move lower, FITBI's yield will also decrease. We do not think the securities will be called and think the risk/rewards are balanced here; hence, a 'Hold' rating.

data.title: SCHD: Enjoy The Dividends As Trumponomics Plays Out
data.description:
SCHD ETF: Relative underperformance likely to continue

I last covered the Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF™ (NYSEARCA:SCHD) on Aug. 1 with an article titled “SCHD Vs. Federal Reserve: My Notes On Dividends And Rates.” That article was motivated by the Fed’s rate decision at that time and rated SCHD as a hold.

Since then, there have been a few material developments in terms of macroeconomics (like the Fed’s updated dot plot) and also geopolitics. In the remainder of this article, I will focus on the new developments of President Donald Trump’s economic policies. In particular, I will present my thoughts on the administration’s broad protective tariffs and the potential extension of corporate tax cuts as reflected in the legislative package known as the "One Big Beautiful Bill Act." I will explain why these developments could have a conflicting impact on SCHD, thus clouding the return prospects of the ETF and likely to cause continued underperformance relative to the broader market.

SCHD ETF: Introduction and exposure

In case there are readers new to SCHD, it is one of the most popular ETFs among dividend investors – and for very good reasons. The top reasons on my list are Schwab’s brand name, rock-bottom fees, and its sound screening method for its holdings, as quote below.

SCHD Fund Description: SCHD’s index focuses on the sustainability and quality of dividends by screening companies based on four dividend quality attributes. These screens have provided enhanced yield and quality factors to the portfolio, along with distinct sector exposures.

As a result of its above screening methodology, the ETF's top holdings are drastically different from the top names in the broader market, which is heavily dominated by tech mega caps. As seen in the chart below, SCHD’s top holdings are better diversified across various non-tech sectors. Leading the list is Chevron (CVX), which makes up 4.38% of the fund. This is followed by AbbVie (ABBV) (healthcare), Altria Group (MO) (consumer staple), Home Depot (HD) (consumer discretionary), etc. Like the S&P 500 Index (SP500, SPY), the fund is also overall top-heavy with its top 10 holdings collectively accounting for a substantial 41.19% of its total asset.

SCHD ETF: Potential impacts from Trumponomics

Given its indexing strategy, SCHD has vastly different sector weightings from the SP500 (as approximately by the SPY ETF), as shown in the next two charts. The most significant difference is in the Technology sector, as just mentioned, where SPY has a substantial 35.19% exposure compared to SCHD's 9.89%. Conversely, SCHD is heavily weighted toward Energy and Consumer Staple, both with about 19% allocation. In contrast, SPY has a minimal exposure of just 2.95% toward the energy sector and about 5% toward the consumer staple sector.

Given SCHD’s particular exposure, I see mixed impacts from the latest policy developments from the Trump administration. On the one hand, the administration kept pushing for broad protective tariffs. The administration has announced reciprocal tariffs on a wide range of goods from various countries since President Trump took office. As two latest developments since my last writing, the administration also imposed a 50% tariff on goods from India, and the president is lobbying EU for 100% tariffs on China and India. These issues could increase the input costs and compress profit margins for U.S. companies. Of course, the pressure will be felt by the overall economy. But I fear companies that make and sell physical goods (like consumer staple companies) will feel more pressure than companies that make and sell digital products.

On the other hand, the administration also kept making progress on the tax front. A key milestone since my last writing on this front is the legislative package known as the "One Big Beautiful Bill Act." This bill made permanent many of the individual and business tax provisions from the 2017 Tax Cuts and Jobs Act, which would boost after-tax profits for many of SCHD’s companies.

Other risks and final thoughts

Of course, at this point, many of you must be wondering about at least two questions. Won’t the tax cut benefit all companies in the economy, and why do I single out SCHD? It is certainly true that a tax cut means higher after-tax income on average for all companies, but I don’t expect the benefits to be uniform. In particular, I expect the tax cuts to benefit stocks in the SCHD fund more than the overall market (which, again, is dominated by technology stocks). There are many reasons, and here I will limit myself to the top 2.

Companies that make and sell physical goods tend to have higher effective tax rates. An example is shown below comparing the tax rates of CVX (SCHD’s top holding) and Nvidia (NVDA) (SP500’s top holding). As such, a lower tax rate could lead to a larger impact on CVX than NVDA. Furthermore, energy companies and consumer staples companies often have more of their business and revenue concentrated domestically. This can lead to a more direct impacts when the effective tax rates in the U.S. change. In contrast, multinational tech companies’ tax bills tend to have lower correlation with the U.S. tax rates due to their global operations, various tax strategies, and also the nature of their digital products.

As a final note (and upside for SCHD), the fund currently provides an attractive valuation and also a generous yield to keep investors rewarded while waiting for the above uncertainties to play out. To wit, SCHD's TTM Dividend Yield is 3.7%, which is over three times higher than SPY's 1.09%, as seen. Furthermore, SCHD has also demonstrated a superior dividend growth rate over both the three-year and five-year periods than the SP500. Its three-year dividend growth stands at 8.19% compared to SPY's 6.06%, and its five-year dividend growth is even stronger at 10.87% versus SPY's 4.60%. Finally, note that SCHD’s current TTM yield is about 8% above its four-year average, indicating a slight valuation discount. While in contrast, SPY’s current TTM yield is about 20% below its four-year average, indicating a sizable valuation premium in the broader market.

To close, this SCHD reexamination focused on the developments of the Trump Administration’s economic policies, particularly in the area of tariffs and tax cuts. Unfortunately, I see mixed and even conflicting impacts of these developments on SCHD, resulting in a neutral risk/reward curve and a reiteration of my Hold rating.
